title: What is a lambda expression in Java and why is it useful?

answers.junior: A lambda expression is an anonymous function in Java that implements a single abstract method of a functional interface, enabling functional programming paradigms in the language. Lambda expressions were introduced in Java 8 and provide a concise syntax to represent instances of functional interfaces, such as those used in the Streams API, collections processing, and event handling.
answers.middle: The basic syntax is (parameters) -> { body }, where you can omit curly braces for single expressions, like (x, y) -> x + y for a simple addition function. Lambda expressions are useful because they significantly reduce boilerplate code compared to anonymous inner classes, making code more readable and maintainable; for example, Collections.sort(list, (a, b) -> a.compareTo(b)) is far cleaner than creating an anonymous Comparator class. They enable functional composition and higher-order functions, allowing you to pass behavior as arguments to methods, which is fundamental to stream processing and method references like list.stream().forEach(System.out::println).
answers.senior: Lambda expressions work seamlessly with the Streams API to create powerful data transformation pipelines: list.stream().filter(x -> x > 5).map(x -> x * 2).collect(Collectors.toList()) processes collections without explicit loops or intermediate collections. A critical requirement is that lambda expressions can only implement functional interfaces—those with exactly one abstract method—which the compiler verifies at compile time to ensure type safety. Performance-wise, lambdas are compiled to invokedynamic bytecode instructions using method handles, making them as efficient as traditional method calls while providing superior expressiveness.
answers.tricky: The most common pitfall is attempting to modify captured variables from within a lambda; variables must be effectively final (not reassigned after initial assignment) to prevent thread safety and closure issues. In production systems, lambdas work best with functional interfaces from the java.util.function package like Function<T,R>, Predicate<T>, and Consumer<T>, enabling consistent, composable, and testable functional-style code that aligns with modern Java idioms.
